Summary: In HR(+)HER2(−) metastatic breast cancer, the standard of care is evolving to include novel selective estrogen receptor degraders (SERDs) for patients whose tumors harbor ESR1 mutations.
Reference: Bhave, M. A., Quintanilha, J. C. F., Tukachinsky, H., Li, G., Scott, T., Ross, J. S., Pasquina, L., Huang, R. S. P., McArthur, H., Levy, M. A., Graf, R. P., & Kalinsky, K. (2024). Comprehensive genomic profiling of ESR1, PIK3CA, AKT1, and PTEN in HR(+)HER2(-) metastatic breast cancer: prevalence along treatment course and predictive value for endocrine therapy resistance in real-world practice. Breast Cancer Res Treat, 207(3), 599-609. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10549-024-07376-w

Summary: ESR1 amplifications and in‑frame gene fusions occur in ER+ breast cancers, and ESR1 in‑frame gene fusions have been found in at least 1% of metastatic breast cancers; they usually occur in tumors resistant to endocrine therapy. Most ESR1 fusions retain the N-terminal DNA-binding domain and exclude the ER ligand-binding domain by fusing to diverse C-terminal partner genes. These fusion proteins drive estrogen-independent ER transcriptional activity and promote cell proliferation, cell motility, and metastasis. Breast cancer cells and a patient-derived xenograft expressing these fusions remained sensitive to CDK4/6 inhibition, suggesting that adding CDK4/6 inhibitors to adjuvant endocrine therapy may prevent acquisition of these fusions. In ER-positive breast cancers, ESR1 ligand-binding-domain mutations such as Y537S drive estrogen-independent metabolic rewiring. ESR1 Y537S-expressing cells exhibit elevated tricarboxylic acid cycle activity, glucose independence, and reliance on glutamine as an alternative carbon source. Antiestrogens suppressed ER and induced a metabolically dormant, self‑renewal‑deficient state in tumors. Chronic fulvestrant exposure increased paracrine IL‑6. Paracrine IL‑6 elevated oxidative phosphorylation to restore growth in quiescent CD133hi/ERlo/Mito lo cells in an ER‑independent manner. PROTACs are hetero-bifunctional small molecules that recruit an E3-ubiquitin ligase to the target protein and have the potential to degrade both wild-type and mutant ESR1 by binding to a region shared between mutant and wild-type ER. The PROTAC ARV‑471 degrades Y537S and D538G mutants and inhibits tumor growth in a patient‑derived xenograft harboring ESR1 Y537S. The authors speculate that agents targeting both mutant and wild‑type ER earlier in treatment may prevent acquisition of ligand‑binding domain mutations.
Reference: Hanker, A. B., Sudhan, D. R., & Arteaga, C. L. (2020). Overcoming Endocrine Resistance in Breast Cancer. Cancer Cell, 37(4), 496-513. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ccell.2020.03.009

Summary: In ER-positive breast cancer cell lines cultured long-term in hormone-depleted media, ESR1 acquired mutations Y537C in MCF-7 and Y537S in SUM44. Analysis detected a low-frequency pre-existing Y537S subpopulation in parental SUM44, and the absence of Y537C in parental MCF-7 indicates mutations can pre-exist or be acquired depending on cell line background. Tamoxifen- or fulvestrant-resistant long-term treated lines did not acquire ESR1 mutations, supporting that most tumors acquire ESR1 mutations during estrogen withdrawal with aromatase inhibitors. Integrated transcriptomic and cistromic analyses showed mutant ESR1 had enriched chromatin binding and enhanced estrogen-independent transcriptional activity. These long-term estrogen-deprived model results confirm engineered ESR1 mutation studies. Droplet digital PCR (ddPCR) allows more sensitive and reliable detection of ESR1 mutations, and studies have identified a hotspot for ESR1 mutations within the ligand-binding domain using various DNA sequencing methods. Current data indicate acquisition of ESR1 mutations is the most common mechanism of endocrine therapy resistance. Using MSK-IMPACT, metastatic ER-positive tumors showed enrichment of additional driver mutations including KMT2C, NCOR1, and AKT1. These findings suggest ESR1 mutant-positive cancers evolve and acquire distinct mechanisms that affect or result from ER signaling.
Reference: Dustin, D., Gu, G., & Fuqua, S. A. W. (2019). ESR1 mutations in breast cancer. Cancer, 125(21), 3714-3728. https://doi.org/10.1002/cncr.32345

Summary: In PlasmaMATCH Cohort A and in fulvestrant‑treated ESR1‑mutant breast cancer patients, baseline ESR1 Y537S associated with poor outcomes and Y537C associated with good outcomes. Paired ctDNA sequencing identified acquired ESR1 F404 mutations (F404L, F404I, F404V). These F404 mutations occurred only in patients with preexisting activating ESR1 mutations and were acquired in cis with those activating mutations. In silico modeling showed ESR1 F404 contributed to fulvestrant binding to ERα via a pi‑stacking bond and that F404 mutations disrupted this bond. In vitro models with single F404L, E380Q, or D538G showed less sensitivity to fulvestrant. Compound D538G+F404L and E380Q+F404L models were resistant to fulvestrant. Compound ESR1 mutations conferred profound resistance to fulvestrant. Several oral ERα degraders were active against the compound mutant models. The acquisition of F404 mutations likely reflected clonal selection during fulvestrant exposure. ESR1 mutations largely drove the high incidence of acquired mutations in tumors that evolved through fulvestrant therapy. Double ESR1 mutations were acquired after aromatase inhibitor exposure. ESR1‑mutant cancers may co‑occur with other genetic resistance mechanisms.
Reference: Kingston, B., Pearson, A., Herrera-Abreu, M. T., Sim, L. X., Cutts, R. J., Shah, H., Moretti, L., Kilburn, L. S., Johnson, H., Macpherson, I. R., Ring, A., Bliss, J. M., Hou, Y., Toy, W., Katzenellenbogen, J. A., Chandarlapaty, S., & Turner, N. C. (2024). ESR1 F404 Mutations and Acquired Resistance to Fulvestrant in ESR1-Mutant Breast Cancer. Cancer Discov, 14(2), 274-289. https://doi.org/10.1158/2159-8290.cd-22-1387

Summary: ER signalling is a foundational dependency in ER+ breast cancer. Activating ESR1 mutations in the ligand-binding domain arise predominantly in metastatic tumours after aromatase inhibitor exposure and are rare in primary and treatment‑naive metastatic disease. Sequential sampling indicates ESR1 mutations are predominantly acquired under the selective pressure of oestrogen deprivation and are selected by aromatase inhibitor treatment. These acquired ESR1 mutations persist through subsequent therapies. These activating ESR1 mutations are important drivers of acquired resistance to endocrine therapy and they specify tumours that retain dependence on ER in the advanced setting. Next-generation ER-targeted agents were developed to target oestrogen-independent ESR1 mutations. In patients with ESR1‑mutant tumours, elacestrant showed a statistically significant improvement in progression‑free survival and received FDA approval in January 2023 for post‑menopausal women with advanced or metastatic ER+ HER2− ESR1‑mutated breast cancer. Sub-group analyses of the EMERALD, SERENA‑2 and acelERA trials suggested these agents were beneficial relative to the physician’s choice of endocrine therapy mostly in the ESR1‑mutant setting. It remains unknown whether these agents will select for ER reactivation mechanisms or drive tumours to ER independence. Genomic and transcriptomic analyses of tumour biopsies and circulating tumour DNA from patients exposed to next-generation ER-targeted agents will represent the leading edge of ER+ tumour evolution and set the stage for future therapeutic strategies.
Reference: Will, M., Liang, J., Metcalfe, C., & Chandarlapaty, S. (2023). Therapeutic resistance to anti-oestrogen therapy in breast cancer. Nat Rev Cancer, 23(10), 673-685. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41568-023-00604-3
